Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 112 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the segmentation data:
- Node 0: S (sky blue), 3794 pixels - background
- Node 1: W (white), 15 pixels - top bar (timer/HUD)
- Node 2: c (charcoal), 17 pixels - top bar (timer/HUD)
- Node 3: N (light green), 36 pixels - green frame part 1
- Node 4: W (white), 135 pixels - character's body
- Node 5: R (red), 9 pixels - red block 1
- Node 6: N (light green), 27 pixels - green frame part 2
- Node 7: N (light green), 9 pixels - green block
- Node 8: S (sky blue), 9 pixels - sky blue block
- Node 9: R (red), 9 pixels - red block 2
- Node 10: N (light green), 36 pixels - green frame part 3
The green frame is split into multiple nodes (3, 6, 7, 10). The character is Node 4 (white, 135 pixels). The red blocks are Nodes 5 and 9.
Looking at the boundaries:
- Node 3 (N): [[29, 41], [29, 49], [31, 49], [31, 44], [32, 43], [34, 43], [34, 41]] - top part of green frame
- Node 5 (R): [[35, 35], [35, 37], [37, 37], [37, 35]] - red block 1
- Node 6 (N): [[35, 47], [35, 49], [43, 49], [43, 47]] - right part of green frame
- Node 7 (N): [[38, 41], [38, 43], [40, 43], [40, 41]] - green block inside
- Node 8 (S): [[38, 44], [38, 46], [40, 46], [40, 44]] - sky blue block inside
- Node 9 (R): [[41, 35], [41, 37], [43, 37], [43, 35]] - red block 2
- Node 10 (N): [[44, 41], [44, 43], [46, 43], [47, 44], [47, 49], [49, 49], [49, 41]] - bottom part of green frame
The green frame is split into 3 parts: top (Node 3), right (Node 6), and bottom (Node 10). There's also a green block inside (Node 7).
The red blocks are at [[35, 35], [35, 37], [37, 37], [37, 35]] and [[41, 35], [41, 37], [43, 37], [43, 35]]. These are to the left of the green frame.
The character is Node 4 (white, 135 pixels). The character's body is to the right of the red blocks.
Looking at the image, the character is horizontal facing left, with the red feet on the left. The green frame is to the right of the character. The green frame has an opening on the left.
I think the goal is to have the character's red feet align with the red blocks. The red blocks are to the left of the green frame. If the character is horizontal facing left, the red feet would be on the left, which could align with the red blocks.
But I've been trying this for 112 actions without success. Let me think about what I'm missing.
Actually, I just realized something. The green frame has an opening on the left. The character is horizontal facing left, with the red feet on the left. The red blocks are to the left of the green frame.
Maybe the goal is to have the character enter the green frame from the left opening, with the red feet leading. The character is horizontal facing left, with the red feet on the left. If I move RIGHT, the character would move towards the green frame. The red feet would enter the green frame from the left opening, with the body trailing.
But the character is horizontal facing left, with the red feet on the left. If I move RIGHT, the character would move to the right. The red feet would move to the right, towards the green frame. The body would follow.
So the red feet would enter the green frame from the left opening, with the body trailing. This seems consistent with the green frame's opening on the left.
Let me try moving RIGHT to get the character into the green frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Character is horizontal facing left (red feet on left)
# Green frame is to the right with opening on left
# Red blocks are to the left of green frame
# Try moving RIGHT to enter green frame from left opening
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
